text: From BBC Learning English.

This is Learning English from the News,
our podcast about the news headlines.
In this programme:

Experts say the world is closer
than ever to disaster.
Hello, I'm Pippa.
And I'm Becca.

In this programme,
we look at one big news story

and the vocabulary in the headlines
that will help you understand it.

You can find all the vocabulary
and headlines from this episode,

So let's hear more about this story.
A famous group of experts have warned

that the world is the closest
it has ever been to disaster.

They've set the Doomsday Clock
at 85 seconds to midnight.

The Doomsday Clock was developed by
an organisation called the Bulletin

of Atomic Scientists to warn about
dangerous technologies and risks
in the world.
Doomsday means the end of the world,

or a time when
something very bad will happen.

The idea is that the closer
the Doomsday Clock is to midnight,

the closer humanity is
to being destroyed.

The Doomsday Clock was set up in 1947 as
a way to track the risk of nuclear war.

Scientists now consider other threats
like climate change
and artificial intelligence,
and update the clock every year.

Scientists move the clock forward
four seconds on Tuesday,

indicating that the risk
of disaster is getting worse.

Let's have our first headline from
The Independent, a British news website.

Doomsday Clock moves closer to midnight
in stark apocalypse warning.

That headline, again
from the independent Doomsday Clock

moves closer to midnight
in stark apocalypse warning.

This headline is about
the decision to change the clock

to warn people
about the risks to the world.

Apocalypse means the end
of the world,

or a very serious event which
will cause destruction and change.

We're interested
in the adjective stark.
Stark means clear or obvious.

We can use stark to emphasise
that something is serious.
So in the headline,

the warning from scientists who set
the Doomsday Clock is stark.
It's clear and serious.

Stark can also be used in a slightly
different way to talk about a place.

If a space like a room is stark,
it means it's quite empty or bare.

Stark is a word you'll often see
in news stories and written down.

Another common use is to talk about a
stark contrast or a stark comparison.
We've had stark, clear.
Often serious.

For example, this accident is
a stark reminder of why health
and safety policies are so important.

from the News. Our podcast
about the news headlines.

We're talking about the latest update
to the Doomsday Clock,

a measure of the risk
to the future of humanity.

The Doomsday Clock
is updated every year

when experts began
making predictions in 1947.

They set the clock at seven minutes
to midnight.
It is now at 85 seconds to midnight,

which scientists say is
because of wars around the world,

the risks of climate change and
of artificial intelligence.

The Doomsday Clock can also
be moved backwards if experts believe
the risks to humanity have reduced.
For example, in 1991,

a t the end of the Cold War,
when the US

and the Soviet Union agreed to reduce
their numbers of nuclear weapons.

The headline says
the world holds its breath.

I'm guessing
this isn't literally, Becca.

You're right, Pippa,
if you literally hold your breath,

you stop breathing
for a short amount of time.

For example, if you swim underwater,
you might hold your breath.

Metaphorically, we use the expression
hold your breath to talk

about a situation
when you're waiting for something,

and perhaps you're nervous
or excited to see what happens.

Yes, we can use the expression
hold your breath.

In situations where the outcome might
be unknown, we usually use it to talk

about the moments
just before we find out an outcome.
In the headline.
The world holds its breath.

The world is waiting nervously
to find out about the Doomsday Clock.
This story is about waiting

for a serious thing
that might have a negative outcome,

but we can also use hold your breath
for exciting or positive situations too.

For example, if you do an exam and
you're about to receive your results,

you might hold your breath
and hope that you passed.

We've had:
Hold your breath.
Wait nervously or excitedly.

For example, the crowd held its breath
as the gymnast began his routine.

This is Learning English from The News,
our podcast about the news headlines.

Today we're talking
about the Doomsday Clock and

how experts have warned of the highest
risk yet to the future of humanity.

As we've mentioned, experts set

Midnight represents the end

Let's have another headline.
This is from BBC News.
Every second counts.

Doomsday clock moves closer
to midnight.

And that headline again:
Every second counts.

Doomsday clock moves closer
to midnight.
This headline is about

what the experts who set the Doomsday
Clock said about their decision.

We're looking at this expression
every second counts.
Yes. Let's break it down.

We know that seconds are
small units of time.
There are 60 seconds in every minute.
Right. And the verb count,

if something counts in this context,
it means it matters or it's important.

So the expression
every second counts.
It means every second is important.

This is a clever phrase because
obviously this story is about a clock.

So every second
on the Doomsday Clock matters

because it's a metaphor
for the risk of disaster.

But also, according to the scientists
involved in this report,

if we want to avoid disaster, action
needs to happen to reduce the risks.

And every second is important
in taking this action.

We use every second counts
in personal contexts,
not just for global problems.

It's just a way to say
that time moves quickly
and that we should use every moment.

For example, My niece is growing up
so fast - every second with her counts.

You can make a similar phrase
with any unit of time.
For example, every minute counts,

every day counts,
every moment counts.
We've had every second counts.
Every moment is important.
For example,

If we're going to finish the project
in time, every second counts.
